main_summary.summary: Folk musician Pete Seeger, interviewed July 22, 2011 in Beacon, NY by Joe Mosnier for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts his decades of involvement in the movement through song. Seeger describes the September 1949 Peekskill, NY attacks on Paul Robeson concerts and his belief that exposure of those attacks blocked J. Edgar Hoover's mass-internment plans. He recalls visiting Highlander Folk School with Woody Guthrie in 1940, then returning in 1957 for the school's 25th anniversary, where he met Martin Luther King Jr., Ralph Abernathy, and Rosa Parks and sang "We Shall Overcome" for them. He traces the song's transmission: Zilphia Horton taught it to him in 1946 in NYC; he printed it in People's Songs; Guy Carawan and Frank Hamilton later set it in 12/8 time at Highlander's 1960 "Singing in the Movement" weekend, after which SNCC adopted it. Seeger discusses the Montgomery bus boycott's origins through E.D. Nixon, calling Eugene "Bull" Connor's televised violence the catalyst that turned Northern public opinion. He describes the 1965 Selma-to-Montgomery march, Mississippi Freedom Summer (including learning of the Goodman-Schwerner-Chaney murders during a church concert), the Freedom Singers' founding through Bernice Johnson Reagon and Cordell Reagon, and the 1967 UN peace rally the day after King's Riverside Church speech. Closing reflections cover Obama, Rutherford B. Hayes, Santa Clara County v. Southern Pacific, and the Spirit of Beacon Day.
key_themes: Music as civil rights organizing tool
main_summary.historical_significance:
Seeger is a uniquely positioned narrator: a participant in the labor-Left song movement of the 1930s and 40s who carried that organizing tradition into the Black freedom struggle through his Highlander connections. His firsthand account of how "We Shall Overcome" moved from Zilphia Horton's teaching in 1946 to the rhythmic reworking by Guy Carawan and Frank Hamilton in 1960 to SNCC's adoption in Raleigh is the most detailed primary-source narration on record of the song's institutional path into the movement.

The interview also documents the 1949 Peekskill attacks from a participant-witness perspective, including Seeger's interpretation that public revulsion at the televised Peekskill mobs disrupted Hoover's planned internment program. His recollections of Highlander's 1957 anniversary, the 1965 Selma march, Mississippi Freedom Summer concerts, and his involvement with the Freedom Singers add layered detail to the historical record of cultural workers within the movement.

chapters.title: Peekskill 1949 Robeson Attacks
chapters.summary: Seeger recounts the September 1949 attacks on Paul Robeson's two Peekskill, NY concerts. He argues that J. Edgar Hoover had already prepared concentration camps for roughly 12,000 people and that President Truman had sent a high official (probably the Secretary of State) to address the American Legion in July 1949 before the violence. Seeger credits public exposure of the mob attacks with derailing Hoover's mass-internment plans.

chapters.title: Highlander 1957 Anniversary
chapters.summary: Seeger describes first visiting Highlander Folk School in 1940 with Woody Guthrie at Alan Lomax's suggestion, then returning in 1957 for the school's 25th anniversary after Zilphia Horton's death. Myles Horton invited him to sing. Martin Luther King Jr., Ralph Abernathy, and Rosa Parks drove up from Montgomery; the group was photographed in front of the barn. This was Seeger's first meeting with King.

chapters.title: King at the UN Peace Rally
chapters.summary: Seeger's second and final meeting with King occurred a year before King's assassination, at an 80,000-person peace rally outside the United Nations the day after King's Riverside Church "Beyond Vietnam" speech of April 4, 1967. Seeger recalls King's words about the United States as "the greatest purveyor of violence in the world" and speculates that Lyndon B. Johnson's reaction to this speech reopened the channel between LBJ and Hoover.
related_events: MLK Riverside Church Beyond Vietnam speech April 4, 1967

chapters.title: Montgomery Bus Boycott Origins
chapters.summary: Seeger describes calling E.D. Nixon at the Montgomery Improvement Association after reading a small New York Times notice about the bus boycott. He relates Nixon's account of why Rosa Parks rather than a pregnant teenager became the test case, Nixon's recruitment of the new minister King to host the meeting at his church, and King's central instruction that the boycott would succeed only through nonviolence. Seeger then turns to Eugene "Bull" Connor, whom he had heard about in 1940 while staying near Birmingham with Joe Gelders.

chapters.title: We Shall Overcome Transmission
chapters.summary: Seeger traces the song's path: Zilphia Horton taught him the song in 1946 in NYC, and he printed it (imperfectly) in People's Songs magazine. The breakthrough came when Guy Carawan and Frank Hamilton learned to perform it in 12/8 time, drawing on Carawan's work with gospel singing at a local Baptist church. At Highlander's 1960 "Singing in the Movement" weekend, the new rhythm became the hit; five weeks later in Raleigh, NC, SNCC adopted both the song and the cross-armed swaying tradition.
related_events: Highlander Singing in the Movement weekend 1960

chapters.title: Carnegie Hall and Freedom Singers
chapters.summary: Seeger recounts his 1963 Carnegie Hall concert, paid for by Columbia Records through John H. Hammond Jr., who had earlier produced the 1938 "From Spirituals to Swing" concert at Carnegie. Seeger describes meeting Bernice Johnson Reagon and Cordell Reagon in Albany, Georgia in 1962, his approach to SNCC's Atlanta office proposing the singers travel like the Fisk Jubilee Singers to raise funds, and Toshi-Aline Ohta Seeger's organizing of the Freedom Singers' first national tour using contacts from his college circuit.

chapters.title: Mississippi Freedom Summer
chapters.summary: Seeger describes flying into Jackson, Mississippi in 1964 with William Kunstler and being confronted at the airport by a passenger who had overheard his remarks on the plane. He recounts performing in a church for 200 people when a note arrived informing him that the bodies of Andrew Goodman, Michael Schwerner, and James Chaney had been found. He sang Fred Hellerman's "Healing River" in response.
related_events: Murder of Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney June 1964

chapters.title: Selma to Montgomery March
chapters.summary: Seeger and Toshi-Aline Ohta Seeger received a telegram from King inviting them to the March 1965 Selma-to-Montgomery march. They joined roughly 300 marchers about a mile outside Selma. Seeger remembers Harry Belafonte urging marchers on, the new way of singing "We Shall Overcome" with the "What do we want? Freedom! When do we want it? Now!" call-and-response that countered the song's "someday" softness, and helicopters watching for snipers over the cornfields.

chapters.title: Late 1960s and King's Final Years
chapters.summary: Asked about the late 1960s, the split in SNCC, and King's expanding focus on economic and foreign-policy issues, Seeger speculates that King knew he would be assassinated and that his final speech was a charge to others to pick up the torch. Seeger reflects on his own uncertainty about humanity's chances and emphasizes the importance of small local efforts.

chapters.title: Spirit of Beacon Day
chapters.summary: Seeger describes a local example of organizing in Beacon, NY: a retired widow and other women created Spirit of Beacon Day after a high school race riot. The annual event closes Main Street and brings together churches, civic clubs, the Beacon Sloop Club, and the River Town Kids music ensemble Seeger taught. Two-thirds of the town now attends; the day grew from a few hundred people to roughly ten thousand.
related_events: Beacon NY high school race riot

chapters.title: Obama, Hayes, and Corporate Power
chapters.summary: Asked about Obama and the long arc of history, Seeger says he wishes he had told Obama in person not to plan for re-election but to do the best job possible and trust contrast with opponents. He then offers a long historical excursus on Rutherford B. Hayes, the contested 1876 election, the federal-troops withdrawal compromise, and the 1886 Santa Clara County v. Southern Pacific Railroad decision, citing Hayes's later observation that the country had become "a government of corporations, by corporations, for corporations."
related_events: Santa Clara County v. Southern Pacific Railroad 1886

chapters.title: Dairy Strike Puppets and Closing
chapters.summary: Seeger describes Camp Nitgedaiget (later Camp Beacon), a 1920s radical summer camp half a mile away founded by Jewish garment workers, where he and three others developed a puppet troupe in the 1930s. Their skit on farmers underpaid for milk had already been written when the New York Dairy Farmers Union strike broke out. The interview closes with reflections on Fannie Lou Hamer and Medgar Evers as exemplars of movement heroism.

loc_video.caption: Pete Seeger oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Beacon, New York, 2011 July 22
